description: Upload and execution of malicious files due to weak validation controls.

complexity_factors.question: Was the uploaded file a webshell giving the attacker persistent remote access?
complexity_factors.reason: A webshell provides ongoing remote command execution. Containment requires finding and removing the shell, not just blocking the upload endpoint.
complexity_factors.source: MITRE T1505.003 — Web Shell: persistent server-side access significantly increases containment complexity

complexity_factors.question: Was the malicious file disguised as a legitimate file type (e.g. .php disguised as .jpg)?
complexity_factors.reason: Type disguise reduces the effectiveness of extension-based detection and requires deeper content inspection to identify.
complexity_factors.source: OWASP A04:2021 — Insecure Design: file type bypass is a common evasion technique reducing detectability

complexity_factors.question: Was the uploaded payload actually executed on the server?
complexity_factors.reason: Execution means the attack moved from upload to active exploitation. The blue team must now treat this as a full compromise, not just a failed upload.
complexity_factors.source: MITRE T1505.003 — Execution of uploaded payload escalates severity from attempt to confirmed compromise

complexity_factors.question: Did the attacker attempt privilege escalation after upload?
complexity_factors.reason: Privilege escalation after upload means the blast radius extends beyond the web application to the underlying OS.
complexity_factors.source: MITRE T1068 — Exploitation for Privilege Escalation: compounds containment effort across application and system layers

ttc_ranges.source: NIST SP 800-61 Rev2 — Section 3.3: Low severity incidents should be contained rapidly with minimal escalation
NIST SP 800-61 Rev2 — Section 3.3: Medium severity incidents require analysis before containment
NIST SP 800-61 Rev2 — Section 3.3: High severity incidents involve multiple IR phases before containment is possible

rules.description: Alert must be investigated within 10 minutes of being raised
rules.trigger: No alert_investigation_started event within 600 seconds of alert_raised
rules.source: NIST SP 800-61 Rev2 — Section 3.2.4: Initial analysis should begin promptly after detection

rules.description: Incident must be confirmed — malicious upload must be escalated
rules.trigger: No incident_confirmed event in event log
rules.source: NIST SP 800-61 Rev2 — Section 3.2: Incident declaration is a required step in the IR lifecycle

rules.description: Containment must be initiated before max_tolerance threshold
rules.trigger: No containment_initiated event before max_tolerance_min seconds
rules.source: NIST SP 800-61 Rev2 — Section 3.3: Containment strategy must be selected and implemented promptly

rules.description: Containment must succeed — uploaded file removed and execution blocked
rules.source: NIST SP 800-61 Rev2 — Section 3.3: Containment must be confirmed before incident can be closed

scenarios.label: Webshell upload — good response
scenarios.description: Blue team detects malicious upload, confirms webshell, removes it and blocks the endpoint within acceptable time.

logs.detail: Wazuh rule 31160 fired — malicious file upload detected in multipart request
Analyst-1 opened alert and reviewed upload logs
Analyst-2 confirmed uploaded file is a PHP webshell disguised as image
Analyst-3 initiated removal of webshell and disabled upload endpoint

scenarios.label: Executed payload — failed containment
scenarios.description: Blue team detects upload but payload executes before containment. Auto-fail triggered due to missing containment_succeeded.

logs.detail: Wazuh rule 31161 fired — suspicious file execution after upload
Analyst-1 attempted to block endpoint but payload already executed
Containment failed — webshell already active on server
